actions.thought: Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. Even on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

J3 suited is a very weak hand, particularly out of position. With no prior reads on the Big Blind's tendencies, stealing from the Small Blind with such a weak holding is generally unprofitable. It's best to fold and wait for better opportunities in position or with stronger cards.

We are 3-handed and first to act on the Button. We hold 9To, which is a marginal offsuit connector. While it can sometimes be opened as a steal in a 3-handed game, folding is a very standard and safe play early in the tournament without reads, as it plays poorly post-flop when called and can easily be dominated.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has already folded. Although I only need to call 50 more chips to see the flop, 53o performs poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. In this tournament setting, it is better to fold such junk hands and wait for better spots rather than bleeding chips unnecessarily.

In a 3-handed game, the Button has a wide opening range to take advantage of position. While King-Deuce offsuit is a relatively weak hand, it is strong enough to attempt a steal against the blinds. A standard 2.5x raise is appropriate here to test the opponents' ranges without overcommitting chips.

My hand, 9hJs, is relatively weak and performs poorly out of position, especially against a Button raise. With the Big Blind still to act, calling would put me in a disadvantaged position post-flop without a strong range advantage. It is disciplined to fold here and preserve my stack for better opportunities.
I have 53 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button raise. This is a very weak hand with low equity and terrible playability post-flop. The clear decision is to fold.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wider opening range. 58s is a speculative hand that plays well post-flop, and opening for a standard raise of 2.5x the big blind (250) is the optimal way to test my opponents' resistance and attempt to take down the blinds.

We have Jack-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 250 (2.5x BB). J9o is simply too weak to defend out of position against a raise, especially with the Big Blind still left to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 2s. While the hand is suited, 72 is the weakest starting hand in poker. Flashlight raised from the Button, which usually indicates a wide range, but defending OOP with 72s is mathematically poor even with the 2.67:1 pot odds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J3 suited is a very weak hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. Opening it as a steal attempt is possible, but generally unnecessary and leads to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-6 offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind to contend with. Although it only costs 50 more to call, 9-6 offsuit is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position post-flop. Given we are early in the tournament, I prefer to fold this marginal holding rather than play a potentially difficult pot out of position.

I am on the button with K3o. In a three-handed game, K3o is a borderline opening hand. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 6 of 150) and the fact that I recently raised and won with a similar hand (Kc2d in Hand 3), I will opt to fold here to maintain a balanced image and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if called, especially since I am at the start of the 150-hand match.

With Jack-Four offsuit, my hand is too weak to contest the pot, even against a single opponent in the Big Blind. While I have already posted the small blind, completing or raising with such a marginal hand is negative expected value. Preserving my stack early in the tournament is a priority, so I will fold.

I have Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button gives me the best position to control the action. I want to build the pot and define my hand strength early by raising to 300, which is a standard opening size.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, QTo is too weak and easily dominated to call out of position. Additionally, the Big Blind is yet to act and could squeeze. Folding is the most standard and profitable long-term play here.

I have pocket eights in the Big Blind. The button raised to 300. In a 3-handed game, pocket eights is a very strong starting hand. While I could call to see a flop, 3-betting for value and to protect my equity against a wide button opening range is the standard play here. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on the opener and potentially take the pot down pre-flop.

With Ace-King offsuit in a three-handed game, I am facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my open. AKo is too strong to fold and has significant equity against almost any 3-betting range. By 4-betting, I define the strength of my hand, seize the initiative, and capitalize on my position. A 4-bet to 2400 puts pressure on the opponent and sets the pot up for a continuation bet on most boards or a profitable stack-off if they commit further.

Flashlight has responded to my 3-bet with a significant 4-bet to 2400. In a 3-handed game, ranges are generally wider, but a 4-bet still signals a very strong hand like high pocket pairs or big broadway cards (AK, AQ). My pocket eights have about 35-40% equity against a standard 4-bet range. I need to call 1400 more into a pot that will be 4850, giving me pot odds of approximately 29%. Since my equity exceeds the pot odds, calling to see a flop is mathematically justified. I will call and see if I can hit a set or if the flop comes low, though I must be prepared to fold if the board is coordinated with overcards and Flashlight continues to show aggression.

On this board of 4c 4s Qd, I am out of position against the pre-flop 4-bettor. A 4-bet range often includes hands like AA, KK, QQ, and AK. The Queen on the board hits a significant portion of that range (QQ, AQ). My pocket eights are now an underpair to the board. Leading into the aggressor is not advisable here, as I want to see how they react to this texture. Checking is the most standard and prudent play to evaluate their strength and potentially realize my equity or get to showdown cheaply if they check back with hands like AK or JJ.

The board is [4c, 4s, Qd], which is very dry and unlikely to have helped my range significantly, though it interacts with the opponent's calling range (which may contain pocket pairs or a queen). Given that I am the 4-bettor, I have range advantage, but holding AK high on a board that contains a queen and paired fours makes this a classic check-back scenario. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (a Queen) or fold out hands I currently beat (lower pocket pairs). Checking allows me to control the pot size, maintain my position advantage, and potentially realize my equity on the turn for free.

The board is 4c 4s Qd 7d. The pot is already large due to the pre-flop 4-bet. When Flashlight checked back on the flop (Qd 4 4), it signaled either a very strong hand (like QQ or AA) playing slow, or more likely, a hand that missed or has marginal showdown value (like AK, JJ, or TT). My pocket eights have showdown value against their missed broadways. Leading here doesn't achieve much as I'll likely only be called by better hands (AQ, KK, AA) and fold out hands I currently beat. I will check to keep the pot controlled and see if I can reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from hands like AK.

I have Ace-King high on a board of 4c-4s-Qd-7d. I checked back the flop to control the pot and maintain my position. On the turn, the 7d does not change the strength of my hand relative to the opponent's 4-bet calling range, which likely includes pocket pairs or a Queen. Since the opponent checked again, I will continue to check back. Betting here is unlikely to generate value from worse hands, and I would be putting myself in a difficult spot if the opponent decides to check-raise or continue on the river. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a showdown cheaply.

The board has tripled the four (4c 4s Qd 7d 4d), meaning anyone with a pocket pair has a full house (Fours full of [Pair]). My pocket eights give me a full house (Fours full of Eights). However, looking at the action, Flashlight 4-bet pre-flop. Their range for 4-betting typically includes high pocket pairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT, 99) and AK. On this board, AA, KK, JJ, TT, and 99 all make a higher full house than mine. If they have a Queen (like AQ), they have Fours full of Queens. If they have AK, they only have three of a kind (Fours) with an Ace kicker. Since Flashlight checked the flop and turn, they are either trapping with a massive hand or they have a marginal hand/missed broadway. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse (AK), and I will likely only be called or raised by hands that beat me (99+ or a Queen). Checking is the safest play to reach showdown and see if my eights are good against their missed high cards.

The board features three 4s, making it highly likely that any pocket pair or a Queen in the opponent's range has me beaten. Given that I have only Ace-high, there is no value in betting because I will only be called by better hands. Checking behind is the most prudent play to reach a showdown cheaply, as I have no realistic way to extract value or force a fold from a better hand at this point.
T2o is a very weak offsuit hand. Despite being on the Button, it is not strong enough to open the pot with a raise or a call, so folding is the correct and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand for a heads-up situation against the Big Blind after the Button folded. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the hand and build the pot. I will put in a standard raise to 3x the big blind.

I am in the big blind with Queen-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with no playability post-flop. Given that Flash has raised from the small blind, continuing out of position against a range that likely dominates mine would be a poor investment of chips. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 4c 2c. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in the deck. In a 3-handed game, I should be aggressive from the button, but 42s is generally below the opening range even in a high-frequency stealing environment. Flashlight (SB) has shown some aggression in previous hands, and Pro (BB) has been folding a lot, but I'd rather wait for a hand with better post-flop playability or blockers to attempt a steal. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with Jack-six suited. The button folded, and I am heads-up against the big blind. While the hand is not particularly strong, the pot odds provided by the small blind investment make calling to see a flop a reasonable strategic decision, especially since I have position post-flop against the big blind.

I have King-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind limped in. While K9o is strong enough to raise and pressure the limp, checking is a safe and solid play. It allows me to see a free flop in position without bloating the pot with a marginal offsuit hand. I will evaluate the flop and use my positional advantage.

The board is Ad 7c 7d, which is a paired board that likely interacts with the Big Blind's wide range. With Jack-high, I have essentially no showdown value and no equity against any Ace or 7. Betting here would be a bluff into a range that is likely to hold at least a piece of this board. Checking is the most prudent action to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

The flop comes Ace-7-7 and Flashlight checks. I have King-high with the 9 of diamonds, offering very limited backdoor equity. Since Flashlight limped pre-flop, their range contains plenty of weak Aces, small pocket pairs, and flush draws that will not fold to a single bet. Checking back controls the pot, allows me to realize my equity for free, and gives me the option to re-evaluate on the turn if it checks through again.

The board shows an Ace, a pair of sevens, and a six. The turn card (6d) gave me a pair of sixes. Given that the action has been checked through on the flop, there is a possibility that my opponent has a weak hand, but a pair of sixes is still very vulnerable against any Ace or seven. Betting here offers no value as I would likely only be called by better hands. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see a free river card, hoping to reach a cheap showdown.

Flashlight has checked to me again on the turn. The board now has three diamonds, and I hold the 9 of diamonds, giving me a 9-high flush draw. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, my draw isn't particularly strong, and there are many higher diamonds that Flashlight could be holding or trapping with. Checking behind allows me to see a free river card, controlling the pot size and avoiding unnecessary risk with marginal equity.

The board is Ad 7c 7d 6d 5h. I hold a pair of sixes. Given that the action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, it is highly likely the opponent also has a marginal hand. Betting would serve little purpose as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and checking allows me to realize my showdown value against a range of potential missed draws or weak pairs. Therefore, I will check to see the opponent's hand.

We are on the river with King-high and the 9 of diamonds after it checks through pre-flop, flop, and turn. The pot is minimal at 200 chips. Flashlight's line suggests a very weak range, possibly a missed draw, weak Ace, or low pair. King-high actually has some showdown value against missed draws like Q-high or J-high. Betting as a bluff might occasionally fold out bottom pair, but it risks running into a sticky weak pair or trap for very little reward. I will check behind and realize my showdown value.
My hand, 3h-8d, is very weak and lacks potential. With no reason to contest the pot, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous hand.
T3o is a very weak hand. Playing it out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding to preserve chips.

We are on the button (first to act pre-flop in a 3-handed game) with King-Seven offsuit. This is a marginal holding that is easily dominated by better Kings and doesn't play particularly well post-flop. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, the best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot to steal or play a pot.

I have a suited King (Kh 5h) in the Small Blind, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially when the button has folded. Raising is superior to limping here to take initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate to exert pressure on the Big Blind.

I hold 9-4 offsuit in the big blind, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability post-flop. With an opponent opening from the small blind, defending this hand would be statistically unprofitable and unnecessary, as it does not have the strength or equity to continue. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 6s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, 63o does not play well post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify a raise or a call against the blinds, especially since I've already shown some aggression from this position in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the small blind with 63 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. Even though the button folded and the cost to call is only 50 chips to potentially win a 150 pot, playing out of position with such a poor hand is generally negative expected value. I will fold and save my stack for a better spot.

K6o is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in a 3-handed game. Although the button provides position, this hand is easily dominated by any calling range from the blinds. As a professional, I prioritize disciplined play over speculative steals, so folding is the most profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack.
Button folded, making it blind vs blind. My hand is 65o, which is too weak to play from the small blind. The best option is to fold and save my chips for a better hand.
I am on the Button with 9h3s, which is an extremely weak, unplayable hand. In a 3-handed game, the Button is first to act pre-flop. The clear correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine suited (Jd 9d) and the Button has folded. This is a strong hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, as this hand plays well post-flop and has good equity.

I hold Jack-Ten suited, which is a strong hand with good playability against a Small Blind raise. The pot odds (2:1) justify a call here to see a flop. Given I am out of position, keeping the pot size controlled is prudent, so I will call the raise of 200 to complete my total commitment of 300 chips.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have two overcards plus a backdoor diamond draw on a relatively dry, low board. A continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on the Big Blind and represent a stronger range (like overpairs or high Aces). I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative.

I hold Jack-Ten suited, which provides two overcards and a backdoor flush draw on this dry board (2-4-7). With a pot of 900 and a bet of 300 to call, I am getting 3:1 pot odds, which is sufficient to continue. Being in position as the Big Blind against the Small Blind, I can comfortably call to see a turn card while maintaining pot control.

The turn card is a four, which pairs the board (2s 4h 7s 4c). I still only have Jack-high. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, the pairing of the board doesn't improve my hand and often makes an opponent less likely to fold if they have any piece of the board or a pocket pair. I will check here to control the pot size and see how Flashlight reacts. If they check back, I get a free river card. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have no showdown value or significant draws.

The turn has been checked to me by the Small Blind. With Jack-Ten suited on a 2-4-7-4 board, I have backdoor flush potential but very little showdown value against a betting range. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity with a free card on the river and keeps the pot size manageable, as betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat.

The river is a King, which is an overcard to the board but also a card that could have potentially improved my range if I were slow-playing or pot-controlling on the turn. However, I currently hold only Jack-high, which has almost zero showdown value. Looking back at Hand #7, Flashlight played very passively with a pocket pair (88) on a similar board, checking all the way to showdown. This suggests Flashlight is a 'sticky' player who is reluctant to fold marginal hands but also unlikely to bet them for value. Since my hand cannot win at showdown, my only options are to bluff or check-fold. Given Flashlight's passive tendencies, a bluff is risky as they may call with any pair or even Ace-high. I will check and concede the pot unless they also check and I somehow have the best hand (highly unlikely).

With a board of 2-4-7-4-K and holding Jack-high, I have very little showdown value. The opponent checked to me, signaling they are likely giving up on a missed draw or have a weak hand. Betting would be unwise as it would likely only be called by hands that beat me, and checking behind allows me to reach showdown and potentially win if the opponent's hand is worse than mine.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours (4c 4s) in a 3-handed game. Pocket pairs are high-value starting hands in short-handed play, and having the button provides a significant positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately or play the pot in position.

AJo is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. The button has been opening frequently, and 3-betting is the optimal aggressive play to isolate the button, take initiative, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position. A raise to 1000 is a standard size to put pressure on the button.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 7c, facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. 87o is a very weak hand, especially out of position against a 3-bet range. Folding is the only viable option.

I have a pocket pair of fours on the button. I opened for 300 and faced a 3-bet to 1000 from Flashlight in the small blind. With the pot odds being 2:1 (calling 700 to win 1400) and having position for the rest of the hand, this is a standard call to see a flop. The effective stack is about 7200, giving us approximately 10:1 implied odds for set-mining, which is acceptable in a 3-handed game where ranges are wider. Folding would be too tight, and 4-betting is unnecessary as I want to keep their bluffs in and minimize losses if they have a higher pair.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a 3-bet pot on a board of King-Queen-Three. While I do not have a pair, I hold an Ace-high hand with a backdoor straight draw. Checking here would likely be perceived as weakness and allow my opponent to take control of the hand. A continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot is standard in this spot to represent strength and force a fold from lower-ranked pocket pairs or unimproved hands that might have called pre-flop.

Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop from the small blind and has now led out with a continuation bet on a board of King-Queen-Trey. This board texture significantly favors a standard 3-betting range, which includes hands like AK, KQ, QQ, and JJ. My pocket fours are now a weak underpair with only two outs to improve. Calling a 700-chip bet into a 2800 pot gives me 4:1 odds, but my actual equity is likely much lower, and I will be forced to fold to any further aggression on the turn or river unless I hit a set. Given the high probability that I am behind and have very few ways to win the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with suited connectors (8h 6h). This is a strong position to open the betting. A raise to 300 puts pressure on the small and big blinds, allowing me to potentially steal the pot or play in position with the initiative.

7c 9h is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, playing this hand out of position in the small blind is unprofitable. The best action is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

KJo is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard button open of 3x. I am getting 2.25:1 odds to call. While 3-betting is an option for value, calling allows me to control the pot size out of position and see a flop with a hand that often dominates the button's wider opening range.

On a board of 10-6-2, I have two overcards with King-Jack. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to them to see how they react to the board texture. Donk-betting here doesn't accomplish much, as I'm likely only getting called by better hands or draws, and I'd rather see a cheap turn or evaluate based on their bet size.

I hold middle pair (sixes) with a backdoor flush draw potential on a board that contains a flush draw (clubs) and overcards (Ten). As the pre-flop aggressor, checking back would give my opponent a free card to realize equity if they are chasing. I will bet 400 to protect my hand and gain value from weaker holdings or draws, maintaining the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. The board is Ten-high (10-6-2) with two clubs. Flashlight, the button raiser, has made a continuation bet of 400 into a 650 pot. I am getting approximately 3.6:1 odds to call. While I only have overcards, in a 3-handed game, my King-high still holds some value against the button's wide opening range. I have 6 clean outs to top pair, which gives me about a 12% chance to improve on the turn. Although I'm slightly behind the direct pot odds required to hit my pair immediately, the price is fair enough to see one more card and see if the aggressor slows down on the turn, especially since my hand can still beat some of their total air bluffs.

I have King-high with two overcards on a relatively dry board. The 3s on the turn doesn't change the board texture significantly. As the out-of-position player who called a flop continuation bet, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see if they continue their aggression or if they check back, which would give me a free river and potentially indicate that my King-high is currently the best hand against their drawing range or air bluffs.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board that has become slightly more connected with the three, offering gutshot possibilities to straight draws (4-5). My opponent check-called on the flop, suggesting they have a hand they are not ready to fold, such as a weak Ten, a flush draw, or a pair of deuces. Betting now risks inflating the pot against a range that may have caught up or is trapping. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep my opponent's bluffs in their range for the river.

The river is a 7 of diamonds, completing a board of 2-6-10-3-7 with no flush or obvious straight possible. I have King-high. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, bet the flop but checked the turn. This check on the turn often indicates a medium-strength hand that wants to get to showdown cheaply or a total air hand they are giving up on. My King-high has very little value as a bet, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. By checking, I can potentially win at showdown if Flashlight was on a missed draw (like clubs) or a weaker high card hand. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold as King-high is a poor bluff-catcher on this runout, but checking allows me the chance to win the pot for free.

The opponent has checked on the river, giving me the opportunity to take the showdown. With middle pair, I have sufficient showdown value against most of the opponent's range, which likely includes missed draws or weak pairs. Betting would only isolate me against stronger hands that would call or raise, while checking back allows me to reach showdown safely and realize my equity.

K9o is a borderline hand, but in a 3-handed game on the Button, it's slightly weak, especially since the blinds have shown a willingness to 3-bet or defend aggressively. Folding is a safe option to conserve our stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Five offsuit. Since the Button has folded, I am only facing the Big Blind. K5o is a reasonable hand to open-raise in a blind-versus-blind situation to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the pot immediately. I have successfully used a 3x opening size from this position in previous hands.

With AcKd in the big blind against a small blind raise, I hold a premium hand and positional advantage. 3-betting to 900 is the optimal strategy to isolate the small blind, build a larger pot, and exert pressure, as my hand plays well both pre-flop and in position post-flop.

I opened with K5o from the Small Blind, but the Big Blind has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call, K5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a 3-betting range. My opponent, Flashlight, has shown aggression in previous hands (Hands #7, #15, #16), indicating they are capable of holding strong hands or applying pressure. Continuing with a dominated King in a bloated pot while out of position is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While I have been active in previous hands, this hand is significantly below the opening range for a three-handed game, especially considering that 'Flashlight' in the Small Blind has shown aggression with 3-bets recently. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

The button (Flash) has folded, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind (Pro). Holding King-Queen offsuit, I have a strong hand for heads-up play. Limping is generally weak, so I will raise to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate the big blind. A raise to 300 is a standard size given the pot size and blind structure.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, K9o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by stronger Kings or better hands in their opening range. Playing it post-flop even with position can be tricky and unprofitable. Folding is the safest and most standard option here.

I am on the button with King-Four suited. This is a standard position to open, as I have the initiative and position. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard steal attempt to take down the 150 in the pot or set up a post-flop advantage if called.

I hold 8d Tc in the small blind, which is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the button, playing this hand out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5c. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25-to-1 to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given Flashlight's recent aggression and my disadvantageous position, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage.
I have pocket Aces, the best starting hand. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and extract value from worse hands.

I am in the small blind with 4d 2c, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. The button (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting some pot odds to call, playing 42o out of position for the rest of the hand is a losing strategy. It has very little post-flop playability and I would be at a significant disadvantage against the range of the button opener. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Defending the big blind with suited connectors like 6c 7c is a standard play against a button open. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), and these hands have strong post-flop potential, especially in position relative to the pre-flop aggressor if I flop a draw or pair.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 7c on a board of Qc 2h Js. I have a flush draw and am out of position against the button. Donk betting into the pre-flop aggressor is generally suboptimal, so I will check to the raiser to see their action and potentially realize my equity or take a free card.

With pocket Aces on a Qc 2h Js board, I have a strong overpair. The board is somewhat coordinated, containing potential straight draws (like AK, KT, T9) and pair+draw combinations that the big blind might have defended with pre-flop. Betting here gets value from worse made hands like top pair (AQ, KQ) or middle pair (AJ, KJ), while also charging draws to see the turn. A bet of around half-pot is well-suited to extract value and maintain control.

The flop of Qc-2h-Js is completely disconnected from my starting hand of 6c-7c. I have no pair, no draw, and no significant equity to continue against a continuation bet. With no realistic chance of winning the pot, folding is the only profitable and disciplined move to conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with QTs, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I want to open for a raise to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage throughout the hand. A standard 3x open is effective here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

4-7 suited is a weak hand, particularly when playing out of position from the small blind against a standard button open. The cost to call (250) is significant relative to the pot, and without a strong post-flop advantage, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision at this stage of the tournament.
